perturbation.original_text: 1.2 "Intellectual Property Rights" as used with respect to a particular ---------------------------- Participant, means all patent rights and/or copyrights (but specifically excluding trademarks and trade secrets) owned or controlled by said Participant (in the sense of having the right to grant rights or licenses hereunder without accounting to third parties) pertaining to Imaging Systems, or any component part thereof, or any method for manufacturing or using the same. "Project Intellectual Property Rights. means patents and patent applications in all countries covering -1- <PAGE> Subject Inventions, as well as copyrights in all countries covering Project Works. "Background Intellectual Property Rights" means all Intellectual Property Rights which are not Project Intellectual Property Rights.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 "Intellectual Property Rights" as used with respect to a particular ---------------------------- Participant, means all patent rights and/or copyrights (including trademarks and trade secrets) owned or controlled by said Participant (in the sense of having the right to grant rights or licenses hereunder without accounting to third parties) pertaining to Imaging Systems, or any component part thereof, or any method for manufacturing or using the same. "Project Intellectual Property Rights. means patents and patent applications in all countries covering -1- <PAGE> Subject Inventions, as well as copyrights in all countries covering Project Works. "Background Intellectual Property Rights" means all Intellectual Property Rights which are not Project Intellectual Property Rights.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifically excludes trademarks and trade secrets from the definition of Intellectual Property Rights. The modified text includes them, creating a conflict within the contract regarding what constitutes Intellectual Property Rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal law defines trademarks as distinct from copyrights and patents, each with its own legal protections. The contract's altered definition blurs these distinctions, potentially misrepresenting rights and obligations concerning trademark usage, protection, and licensing.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts legal definitions by including trademarks and trade secrets within the definition of 'Intellectual Property Rights' where the original text explicitly excluded them. The Law Explanation highlights that federal law treats trademarks, copyrights, and patents as distinct with their own protections. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 15 USC 1127, focuses on definitions related to trademarks, underscoring their distinct legal identity. By blurring these lines, the modified contract introduces a misaligned terminology that makes the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 1.4 "Subject Invention" means any invention which is conceived or first ----------------- actually reduced to practice in the performance of work in connection with the Project.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 "Subject Invention" means any idea which is discussed or first ----------------- written about in the performance of work in connection with the Project.
perturbation.explanation: The original text correctly uses the term 'invention' with the established legal criteria of conception and reduction to practice. The modified text replaces 'invention' with 'idea' and shifts the criteria to 'discussed or first written about,' fundamentally altering the definition and scope of what qualifies as a Subject Invention.
perturbation.law_explanation: U.S. patent law requires an invention to be new, useful, and non-obvious (35 U.S.C. § 101 and § 103). Simply discussing or writing about an idea does not meet the legal threshold for an 'invention' that can be patented or protected as intellectual property. This misdefinition could lead to disputes over ownership of mere ideas that lack inventive merit.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 35 U.S. Code § 101 - Inventions patentable | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 101 - Inventions patentable U.S. Code Notes prev | next Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process , machine, manufacture, or composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent therefor, subject to the conditions and requirements of this title. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 797 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on Title 35, U.S.C., 1946 ed., § 31 (R.S. 4886, amended (1) Mar. 3, 1897, ch. 391, § 1, 29 Stat. 692 , (2) May 23, 1930, ch. 312, § 1, 46 Stat. 376 , (3) Aug. 5, 1939, ch. 450, § 1, 53 Stat. 1212 ). The corresponding section of existing statute is split into two sections, section 101 relating to the subject matter for which patents may be obtained, and section 102 defining statutory novelty and stating other conditions for patentability. Section 101 follows the wording of the existing statute as to the subject matter for patents, except that reference to plant patents has been omitted for incorporation in section 301 and the word “art” has been replaced by “ process ”, which is defined in section 100. The word “art” in the corresponding section of the existing statute has a different meaning than the same word as used in other places in the statute; it has been interpreted by the courts as being practically synonymous with process or method. “ Process ” has been used as its meaning is more readily grasped than “art” as interpreted, and the definition in section 100(b) makes it clear that “ process or method” is meant. The remainder of the definition clarifies the status of processes or methods which involve merely the new use of a known process , machine, manufacture, composition of matter, or material; they are processes or methods under the statute and may be patented provided the conditions for patentability are sa

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts U.S. patent law, specifically 35 U.S.C. § 101, as evidenced by both scraped snippets. The original contract defines 'Subject Invention' using the established legal criteria of 'conceived or first actually reduced to practice.' However, the changed text broadens this definition to include any 'idea which is discussed or first written about.' This modification lowers the threshold for what constitutes an invention, encompassing mere ideas that have not been developed to the point of being patentable. Both snippets explicitly state that 'Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent therefor.' The modified text could potentially lead to disputes over intellectual property rights for ideas that do not meet the statutory requirements for patentability, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 Payments to Participants. As soon as practicable after receipt of ------------------------ monthly payments from NIST, the Project Manager shall reimburse the Participants. In the event that NIST does not reimburse 49.0% of the Consortium's billed costs, each Participant will share proportionately in the shortfall (i.e., in accordance with that Participant's share of the total cost billed) unless any particular Participant is specifically responsible. In such a case, the disallowance will be deducted from the responsible Participant's costs and the reimbursement adjusted accordingly.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 Payments to Participants. The Project Manager shall hold all monthly payments from NIST indefinitely for future project costs. In the event that NIST does not reimburse 49.0% of the Consortium's billed costs, the Project Manager will solely decide how to use those funds.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the Project Manager to reimburse participants promptly. The modified text grants the Project Manager indefinite control over NIST funds, conflicting with the original intent of timely reimbursement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 31 U.S. Code § 6307 - Monitoring of block grants and demonstration projects
perturbation.law_explanation: This code relates to the monitoring of federal funds distributed via block grants or demonstration projects. This section requires accountability and proper dispersal of funds for the project. The modification of Section 5.3 would allow the project manager to hold the funds and use them at their discretion. This potentially impedes the oversight process required by the federal government and makes accountability difficult.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original agreement regarding the disbursement of funds. The original text mandates timely reimbursement to participants after NIST payments, whereas the modified text allows the Project Manager to indefinitely hold and solely decide on the use of these funds. This directly conflicts with the principle of accountability and proper dispersal of funds, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. While the provided scraped law snippet does not explicitly detail fund disbursement for demonstration projects, the law explanation mentions the importance of accountability in federal funds, which is undermined by the contract's modification.

perturbation.original_text: 9.8 Insurance. Each Participant shall obtain and maintain appropriate --------- public liability and casualty insurance, or adequate levels of self insurance, to insure against any liability caused by that Participant's activity under this Agreement and/or the Cooperative Agreement. Within ninety [90] days after this Agreement has been signed by all the Participants, each Participant who is not entirely self insured for matters relating to this Agreement shall obtain from its relevant insurers, and deliver to the other Participants, a written waiver of subrogation for the benefit of the other Participants under each and every purchased insurance policy which said Participant has which is relevant to said Participant's activity under this Agreement. Such a written waiver will also be obtained and promptly delivered for each relevant new or renewal policy obtained by either Participant during the term of this Agreement. In lieu of obtaining such a waiver for a particular policy of its purchased insurance, a Participant may instead arrange to have the other Participants named as additional insureds on said policy. Each Participant shall be responsible for its own self insurance retention's and for all deductible amounts under its purchased insurance.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.8 Insurance. Each Participant should consider obtaining --------- public liability and casualty insurance, or adequate levels of self insurance, to insure against any liability caused by that Participant's activity under this Agreement and/or the Cooperative Agreement. Obtaining a written waiver of subrogation is optional.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that each participant obtain and maintain insurance and provide a waiver of subrogation. The modified text makes obtaining insurance and a waiver of subrogation optional.
perturbation.contradicted_law: FAR 52.228-5 - Insurance - Work on a Government Installation
perturbation.law_explanation: FAR 52.228-5 mandates insurance coverage for work on a Government installation. The modified language weakens the requirement for insurance and a waiver of subrogation, potentially conflicting with government contract requirements for adequate liability coverage. The change introduces ambiguity regarding risk allocation and liability in case of accidents or damages during the project.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates insurance and a waiver of subrogation, while the changed version makes these optional. FAR 52.228-5, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that the contractor "shall, at its own expense, provide and maintain during the entire performance of this contract, at least the kinds and minimum amounts of insurance required in the Schedule or elsewhere in the contract." By making insurance optional, the changed text directly contradicts this mandatory requirement for contracts involving work on a Government installation, creating a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition.
